questions.stem: A client reports a regular menstrual cycle of 35 days. She asks the nurse when she typically ovulates. Which response is most accurate?

options.A: Day 14, because ovulation always occurs on day 14
options.B: Day 21, because the luteal phase is fixed at 14 days from the end of the cycle
options.C: Day 28, because ovulation occurs 7 days before menstruation
options.D: Day 7, because early cycles have earlier ovulation

questions.rationale: The luteal phase is relatively constant at 14 days. Ovulation therefore occurs approximately 14 days before the NEXT menstruation. In a 35-day cycle: 35 − 14 = day 21 of the cycle. The follicular phase (days 1 through ovulation) is the variable portion. Day 14 only applies to a 28-day cycle.

incorrectRationales.A: Day 14 is only correct for a 28-day cycle. Applying day 14 universally to all cycles is a common and dangerous misconception for family planning.
incorrectRationales.C: Day 28 would be only 7 days before the next period — not consistent with a fixed 14-day luteal phase.
incorrectRationales.D: Day 7 would imply a very short follicular phase; this is extremely uncommon.

questions.hint: Luteal phase = fixed 14 days. Ovulation = cycle length − 14.
questions.clinicalPearl: Women with longer cycles (35+ days) who assume they ovulate on day 14 may miss their fertile window by a week. LH testing is essential for accurate ovulation detection in women with irregular cycles.
questions.examStrategy: Always calculate ovulation day as: cycle length − 14 = ovulation day. This formula is testable in clinical scenarios involving fertility counseling.
questions.keyTakeaway: Ovulation = cycle length − 14 days. Luteal phase is constant; follicular phase is variable.

questions.stem: A nursing student asks how oral contraceptive pills (OCPs) prevent ovulation. Which explanation most accurately describes the mechanism?

options.A: Estrogen in OCPs creates a sustained high level that triggers a positive feedback LH surge, which terminates follicular development
options.B: Exogenous estrogen and progestin suppress FSH (preventing follicular development) and progestin suppresses the LH surge
options.C: OCPs work primarily by preventing sperm from reaching the egg through cervical mucus thickening alone
options.D: OCPs create a progesterone-only environment that directly inhibits GnRH release from the ovary

questions.rationale: Combined OCPs prevent pregnancy through multiple mechanisms: (1) exogenous estrogen suppresses FSH → prevents follicular recruitment and dominant follicle development; (2) progestin suppresses the LH surge → prevents ovulation even if a follicle develops; (3) progestin thickens cervical mucus; (4) progestin creates an unfavorable endometrium for implantation. This is a synergistic multi-mechanism approach.

incorrectRationales.A: The OCP estrogen is maintained at a steady low level — it does NOT create the sustained high-level estrogen peak needed for a positive feedback LH surge. The pill's purpose is to suppress the LH surge, not trigger one.
incorrectRationales.C: Cervical mucus thickening is a mechanism, but it is not the primary mechanism — ovulation suppression is the dominant effect of combined OCPs.
incorrectRationales.D: GnRH is released from the hypothalamus, not the ovary. Progesterone acts on the pituitary and hypothalamus to suppress FSH/LH.

questions.hint: COCs: estrogen suppresses FSH (no follicle); progestin suppresses LH surge (no ovulation); also thickens mucus.
questions.clinicalPearl: Missing 3+ consecutive active pills significantly increases ovulation risk. Backup contraception is needed for 7 days after missed pills to account for potential follicular development that occurred during the missed days.
questions.examStrategy: OCP mechanism questions: list all mechanisms in order of importance — ovulation suppression (primary), cervical mucus (secondary), endometrial changes (tertiary). The examiner may test only one or all three.
questions.keyTakeaway: COCs prevent pregnancy through: FSH suppression (estrogen), LH surge suppression + mucus thickening + endometrial changes (progestin).

questions.stem: A client trying to conceive calls the clinic and reports that her LH test strip just turned positive. She asks what she should do. Which response is most appropriate?

options.A: Ovulation has already occurred; she should wait for the next cycle
options.B: She should have intercourse today and tomorrow, as ovulation will occur within 24–36 hours
options.C: She should wait 3 days before intercourse to allow full egg maturation
options.D: She should take a pregnancy test in 3 days to confirm successful ovulation

questions.rationale: A positive LH test strip indicates the onset of the LH surge, which precedes ovulation by 24–36 hours. Intercourse on the day of the positive LH test and the following day maximizes the chance of sperm being present in the tube when the egg is released. The oocyte is viable for only 12–24 hours after ovulation.

incorrectRationales.A: The positive LH strip indicates the surge is just beginning — ovulation has NOT occurred yet. This is the ideal time to act, not to wait.
incorrectRationales.C: Waiting 3 days would miss the fertile window — the oocyte would have degenerated by then.
incorrectRationales.D: An LH strip is not a pregnancy test — it detects the LH surge. A pregnancy test would be used 2 weeks later if a period is missed.

questions.hint: Positive LH = ovulation in 24–36 hours = have intercourse NOW and next day.
questions.clinicalPearl: Sperm remain viable in the female reproductive tract for 3–5 days. Having intercourse slightly before ovulation (when LH strip first turns positive) is actually slightly better than intercourse only on the day of ovulation.
questions.examStrategy: LH strip questions: positive strip = ovulation UPCOMING in 24–36 hours. Action = intercourse that day and next day. Do NOT wait or delay.
questions.keyTakeaway: Positive LH strip → ovulation in 24–36 hours → intercourse that day and next day for maximum conception probability.

questions.stem: A client at 7 weeks gestation is diagnosed with a corpus luteum cyst on ultrasound. Her provider explains that the cyst is important at this gestational age. The client asks why. Which explanation is most accurate?

options.A: The corpus luteum cyst produces hCG, which is the hormone that maintains the pregnancy
options.B: The corpus luteum produces progesterone, which is still critical for maintaining the uterine lining at 7 weeks
options.C: The corpus luteum cyst prevents infection of the ovary during early pregnancy
options.D: The corpus luteum secretes estrogen, which prevents preterm labor in early pregnancy

questions.rationale: At 7 weeks, the placenta is not yet self-sufficient in producing progesterone — the 'luteal-placental shift' occurs at approximately 8–10 weeks. Until then, the corpus luteum remains the primary source of progesterone. Progesterone maintains the decidualized endometrium supporting the embryo. If the corpus luteum were removed before 8–10 weeks without progesterone supplementation, miscarriage could occur.

incorrectRationales.A: hCG is produced by the trophoblast/syncytiotrophoblast, not the corpus luteum. hCG keeps the corpus luteum alive — it is the embryo's signal, not the corpus luteum's product.
incorrectRationales.C: The corpus luteum is not a barrier against infection — it is a hormone-producing structure.
incorrectRationales.D: While corpus luteum produces some estrogen, the clinically critical product at 7 weeks is progesterone (uterine support). Estrogen is not the main answer here.

questions.hint: At 7 weeks: corpus luteum still producing progesterone. Placental takeover at 8–10 weeks.
questions.clinicalPearl: If ovarian surgery is needed before 10 weeks and the corpus luteum must be removed, vaginal progesterone supplementation is prescribed to prevent progesterone deficiency-induced miscarriage.
questions.examStrategy: Corpus luteum questions at early gestation: always emphasize progesterone production. After 10 weeks, the placenta has taken over and the corpus luteum becomes less critical.
questions.keyTakeaway: Corpus luteum = primary progesterone source until 8–10 weeks (luteal-placental shift). Removal before this = miscarriage risk without progesterone supplementation.

questions.stem: During fertilization, only one sperm is able to fertilize the oocyte despite millions of sperm surrounding it. What is the primary mechanism that prevents additional sperm from entering the oocyte after the first sperm penetrates?

options.A: The LH surge creates an enzyme that destroys all remaining sperm in the fallopian tube
options.B: The zona reaction: cortical granule contents released by the oocyte harden the zona pellucida, preventing additional sperm penetration
options.C: The first sperm signals the hypothalamus to immediately stop FSH production, ending gamete viability
options.D: The oocyte physically contracts and closes all zona pellucida channels after first sperm entry

questions.rationale: After the first sperm fuses with the oocyte membrane, the oocyte releases cortical granules (stored just under the plasma membrane) in the cortical reaction. The enzymes released (including ovastacin) modify and harden the zona pellucida in the zona reaction, creating a polyspermy block. Polyspermy (fertilization by >1 sperm) produces a triploid embryo, which is non-viable.

incorrectRationales.A: LH has no role in post-fertilization polyspermy prevention — LH surge occurs hours before fertilization.
incorrectRationales.C: Hypothalamic-pituitary feedback is irrelevant to the polyspermy mechanism at the cellular level.
incorrectRationales.D: The zona pellucida is an acellular glycoprotein matrix — it does not have 'channels' that physically open and close.

questions.hint: Polyspermy block = cortical reaction → zona reaction → hardened zona = only 1 sperm gets in.
questions.clinicalPearl: Polyspermy-derived triploid embryos (69 chromosomes) are recognized as partial moles histologically — one reason molar pregnancies occur even with apparently normal reproductive function.
questions.examStrategy: If asked what prevents polyspermy → cortical reaction → zona reaction → hardened zona pellucida. This is a high-yield embryology concept that bridges to molar pregnancy pathology.
questions.keyTakeaway: Polyspermy prevention: first sperm fusion → cortical reaction → zona reaction (zona hardens) → no additional sperm can penetrate.

questions.stem: A client at 6 weeks gestation has two serum β-hCG levels drawn 48 hours apart: 1,200 mIU/mL and 1,680 mIU/mL. How should the nurse interpret this result?

options.B: Abnormal — levels are not doubling appropriately, suggesting possible ectopic or non-viable pregnancy
options.C: Abnormal — levels are rising too quickly, suggesting multiple gestation
options.D: Normal — any positive rise in β-hCG over 48 hours is acceptable

questions.rationale: In a viable intrauterine pregnancy with β-hCG between 1,000–6,000 mIU/mL, the level should increase by at least 53–66% (approximately double) every 48 hours. From 1,200 to 1,680 represents a 40% rise — below the expected minimum of 53%. This sub-optimal rise suggests a non-viable IUP (failed pregnancy) or ectopic pregnancy, both requiring further evaluation with transvaginal ultrasound.

incorrectRationales.A: A true doubling would be from 1,200 to approximately 2,400. 1,680 is only a 40% rise — below the expected minimum threshold.
incorrectRationales.C: Levels rising 'too quickly' would be >2× in 48 hours — multiples may show this pattern, but 1,680 is actually below the expected rise.
incorrectRationales.D: A positive but sub-optimal rise is not reassuring — it requires further investigation for ectopic or non-viable pregnancy.

questions.hint: β-hCG should rise ≥53% (approximately double) every 48 hours in viable IUP below 6,000 mIU/mL.
questions.clinicalPearl: At very low β-hCG levels (<1,200 mIU/mL), a slightly slower rise may still be compatible with a viable pregnancy. At levels above the discriminatory zone (≥1,500 mIU/mL), no intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS is highly suggestive of ectopic.
questions.examStrategy: β-hCG doubling rules: <53% rise in 48h = concerning (ectopic or non-viable). >200% rise = possible molar pregnancy or multiples. Know the discriminatory zone concept.
questions.keyTakeaway: β-hCG should rise ≥53% every 48h (approximately double). Sub-optimal rise = possible ectopic or non-viable pregnancy → further evaluation needed.

questions.stem: A client who is planning a pregnancy asks when she should start taking folic acid supplements. Which response is most accurate?

options.A: At the first prenatal appointment, usually 8–10 weeks gestation
options.B: As soon as a positive home pregnancy test is confirmed
options.C: At least 1 month before conception (ideally 3 months), and continuing through the first trimester
options.D: Only if there is a family history of neural tube defects

questions.rationale: The neural tube (which becomes the brain and spinal cord) closes by day 28 post-fertilization (approximately week 6 gestational age). Most women do not know they are pregnant at this point. Folic acid supplementation must begin at least 1 month before conception (ideally 3 months) so adequate tissue levels are established before neural tube closure. The recommended dose for average-risk women is 400–800 mcg/day; women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy need 4,000 mcg/day.

incorrectRationales.A: By 8–10 weeks, the critical neural tube closure period (day 28) has already passed — starting folic acid at the first prenatal visit is too late to prevent NTDs.
incorrectRationales.B: A positive pregnancy test is detected at ~4 weeks gestational age. Neural tube closure occurs at ~6 weeks — starting at positive test may be adequate but is cutting it very close. Pre-conception is strongly preferred.
incorrectRationales.D: Folic acid supplementation is recommended for ALL women of reproductive age, not just those with family history. NTDs often occur without a family history.

questions.hint: Neural tube closes by day 28 (week 6) — must have folic acid on board BEFORE that point.
questions.clinicalPearl: The CDC recommends that all women of reproductive age take 400 mcg folic acid daily — whether or not pregnancy is planned — because 50% of pregnancies are unplanned.
questions.examStrategy: Folic acid timing: pre-conception, 1–3 months before, continue through first trimester. Neural tube closes day 28 — if you wait for positive test, you may already be too late.
questions.keyTakeaway: Folic acid: start ≥1 month pre-conception (ideally 3 months); 400–800 mcg/day (standard); 4,000 mcg if prior NTD. Neural tube closes day 28 — can't wait for positive test.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old client requests a combined oral contraceptive pill. During the health history, she reports migraines with visual aura occurring approximately once monthly. What should the nurse do?

options.A: Prescribe the lowest-dose estrogen OCP available to minimize migraine frequency
options.B: Refer to the provider, as migraine with aura is a contraindication to estrogen-containing contraceptives due to increased stroke risk
options.C: Prescribe the OCP and add a daily triptan for migraine prophylaxis
options.D: Reassure her that migraine with aura is only a concern if she is also a smoker

questions.rationale: Migraine with aura is a WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria Category 4 (absolute contraindication) for estrogen-containing contraceptives. Women with migraine with aura have a baseline 2–4× increased stroke risk (from vasospasm and platelet activation during aura episodes). Adding exogenous estrogen (which increases coagulation factors, reduces fibrinolysis, and promotes thrombosis) multiplies this risk synergistically. Progestin-only methods are safe alternatives.

incorrectRationales.A: No dose of estrogen is safe in this situation. 'Lowest dose' does not eliminate the stroke risk — the contraindication applies to any estrogen-containing method.
incorrectRationales.C: Triptans do not mitigate the stroke risk from estrogen in migraine with aura patients. This combination would be dangerous.
incorrectRationales.D: Migraine with aura is an independent absolute contraindication — smoking is a separate additional risk factor, not a required co-factor for the contraindication.

questions.hint: Migraine with aura + estrogen = stroke risk. WHO MEC Category 4 = absolute contraindication.
questions.clinicalPearl: Migraine WITHOUT aura is only a relative contraindication (WHO MEC Category 2 — use with caution, benefits usually outweigh risks). Migraine WITH aura is Category 4 (absolute contraindication). The distinction is critical.
questions.examStrategy: Estrogen contraindications to memorize: VTE history, migraine WITH aura, smoking ≥35 years, uncontrolled HTN, breastfeeding <6 weeks, ischemic heart disease/stroke history, antiphospholipid antibodies.
questions.keyTakeaway: Migraine with aura = absolute contraindication to estrogen-containing contraceptives (stroke risk). Use progestin-only method instead.

questions.stem: A client reports her period lasts 8 days and she soaks 6 pads per day during the first 3 days. She asks if this is normal. Which response is most appropriate?

options.A: Normal menstrual duration is up to 10 days with no volume concern
options.B: The duration of 8 days and heavy flow described warrants evaluation for menorrhagia
options.C: This is completely normal for all women and no follow-up is needed
options.D: Only the duration is abnormal; the volume of 6 pads per day is within the normal range

questions.rationale: Normal menstrual parameters: duration 3–7 days; volume 30–80 mL per cycle (approximately 1–6 pads/day). Eight days exceeds the upper limit of normal duration (>7 days = menorrhagia/abnormal uterine bleeding). Soaking 6 pads/day for 3 days also suggests volume in the upper range. Evaluation for causes (fibroids, polyps, coagulopathy, endometrial pathology, ovulatory dysfunction) is warranted.

incorrectRationales.A: Normal duration is 3–7 days. Eight days exceeds the normal range.
incorrectRationales.C: These parameters are outside the normal range — routine follow-up rather than reassurance is appropriate.
incorrectRationales.D: Both duration and volume are outside or at the upper limit of normal, warranting evaluation.

questions.hint: Normal period: 3–7 days duration, 30–80 mL (2–6 pads/day average). >7 days = abnormal.
questions.clinicalPearl: Menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding) is defined as >80 mL per cycle or duration >7 days. Common causes: structural (fibroids, polyps), hormonal (anovulation, thyroid disease), hematologic (vWD, platelet dysfunction), iatrogenic (anticoagulants, IUD).
questions.examStrategy: Normal menstrual parameters are frequently tested: 21–35 day cycle; 3–7 day duration; 30–80 mL volume. Any parameter outside this range requires evaluation.
questions.keyTakeaway: Normal menstrual duration: 3–7 days; volume: 30–80 mL. Duration >7 days + heavy flow = menorrhagia → evaluate.

questions.stem: A client has been charting her basal body temperature (BBT) for fertility awareness. She notices that her temperature dropped slightly on cycle day 14, then rose 0.4°C and has remained elevated for the past 4 days. How should the nurse interpret this?

options.A: The temperature drop and sustained rise confirm that ovulation occurred approximately 4 days ago
options.B: The temperature changes are normal variation and have no clinical significance for ovulation timing
options.C: The elevated temperature indicates a fever from infection — the client should seek medical care
options.D: The temperature rise predicts ovulation will occur in the next 24–36 hours

questions.rationale: BBT charting interprets post-ovulatory temperature rise: a slight drop (from estrogen peak before ovulation), followed by a sustained rise of 0.2–0.5°C (from progesterone secreted by corpus luteum) that persists for the remainder of the cycle confirms ovulation has occurred. The rise begins 1–2 days after ovulation. Sustained elevation for 3+ days = confirmed ovulation. BBT confirms ovulation retrospectively but cannot predict it prospectively.

incorrectRationales.A: This is actually the correct answer — confirmed above.
incorrectRationales.B: The biphasic temperature pattern is clinically significant — it reflects the progesterone effect of the corpus luteum post-ovulation.
incorrectRationales.C: BBT measurements are taken immediately upon waking before any activity — a rise of 0.4°C reflects progesterone, not infection. Infection would cause a higher fever (>38°C/100.4°F) without the biphasic pattern.
incorrectRationales.D: BBT rise occurs AFTER ovulation, not before. LH strips, not BBT, can predict ovulation in advance.

questions.hint: BBT rise = AFTER ovulation (progesterone effect). Pre-ovulatory drop = estrogen. Rise persisting = corpus luteum active.
questions.clinicalPearl: BBT charting alone has limited utility for timing intercourse because the rise occurs after ovulation. It is best used to confirm that ovulation is occurring and to retrospectively identify the cycle pattern.
questions.examStrategy: BBT questions: temperature rises AFTER ovulation from progesterone. Cannot predict ovulation in advance. Pre-ovulatory drop is from estrogen. Sustained rise = ovulation confirmed.
questions.keyTakeaway: BBT: drops slightly before ovulation (estrogen), then rises 0.2–0.5°C after ovulation (progesterone). Confirms ovulation occurred — cannot predict it in advance.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old client with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) reports cycles occurring every 45–90 days. Transvaginal ultrasound shows >20 small follicles (2–9 mm) per ovary but no dominant follicle. How should the nurse explain the pathophysiology of this finding?

options.A: PCOS causes follicles to grow too quickly, bypassing the dominant follicle selection process
options.B: In PCOS, multiple follicles are recruited but none achieves dominance due to FSH resistance and androgen excess, resulting in follicular arrest at the early antral stage
options.C: The small follicles represent corpus luteum cysts from multiple prior ovulations
options.D: PCOS decreases AMH, reducing the number of available follicles to none at all

questions.rationale: In PCOS, the pathophysiology involves: (1) elevated LH (relative LH/FSH dominance) → excess androgen production by theca cells; (2) insulin resistance → hyperinsulinemia → further androgen stimulation; (3) elevated androgens → impair granulosa cell aromatase → reduced estradiol conversion → insufficient estradiol for dominant follicle selection; (4) multiple small antral follicles accumulate but cannot achieve dominance → follicular arrest (polycystic appearance). AMH is actually ELEVATED in PCOS (from large number of antral follicles).

incorrectRationales.A: In PCOS, follicles grow slowly and arrest early — they do not grow too quickly.
incorrectRationales.C: Corpus luteum cysts are post-ovulatory structures; in anovulatory PCOS, the patient rarely ovulates, making corpus luteum cysts uncommon. The small follicles are arrested antral follicles.
incorrectRationales.D: AMH is elevated in PCOS (from the increased number of small antral follicles), not decreased.

questions.hint: PCOS: elevated LH + androgens → follicular arrest → multiple small follicles, no dominant → anovulation. AMH is HIGH.
questions.clinicalPearl: AMH is typically 2–3× higher than normal in PCOS. This is why PCOS patients have excessive response to ovarian stimulation (OHSS risk with IVF).
questions.examStrategy: PCOS pathophysiology: LH↑ + androgen↑ + insulin resistance → follicular arrest → anovulation. AMH is HIGH (not low). TVUS: 'pearl necklace' appearance of multiple peripheral small follicles.
questions.keyTakeaway: PCOS: multiple small follicles arrest (no dominant follicle) due to LH/androgen excess + insulin resistance. AMH elevated. Result: anovulation, oligomenorrhea.

questions.stem: A client reports severe irritability, depression, and interpersonal conflicts occurring exclusively in the 10 days before her period, resolving completely within 2 days of menstruation onset. This has occurred for 6 consecutive cycles and significantly affects her work and relationships. Which management approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Reassurance that these are normal PMS symptoms requiring only lifestyle modification
options.B: Evaluation for clinical depression and initiation of continuous SSRI therapy
options.C: Evaluation for PMDD; if confirmed, SSRI therapy (continuous or luteal-phase dosing) is first-line
options.D: Recommend an oral contraceptive pill to regulate hormone levels and resolve symptoms

questions.rationale: This presentation meets DSM-5 PMDD criteria: multiple mood symptoms occurring predictably in the late luteal phase (10 days before menses), resolving within days of menstruation onset, documented over multiple cycles, and causing significant functional impairment. PMDD is different from clinical depression (which doesn't resolve with menses). SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram) are first-line — they can be given continuously or only during the luteal phase (days 14–28). Either approach is effective, with luteal-phase dosing reducing side effect burden.

incorrectRationales.A: PMS is milder and does not cause significant functional impairment. This presentation causing work/relationship impact with 10 days of severe symptoms = PMDD, not PMS that requires only lifestyle management.
incorrectRationales.B: Clinical depression persists throughout the cycle and does not reliably resolve with menstruation. Before diagnosing and treating clinical depression, the cyclic pattern must rule out PMDD. Continuous SSRIs may be appropriate for PMDD but the diagnostic step matters.
incorrectRationales.D: OCPs (particularly drospirenone-containing pills like Yaz) are approved for PMDD, but SSRIs are first-line before hormonal contraception for non-contraception-seeking patients.

questions.hint: PMDD: severe luteal-phase mood symptoms that resolve with menses + significant impairment → SSRIs first-line.
questions.clinicalPearl: The hallmark of PMDD is symptom onset in the luteal phase with complete resolution in the early follicular phase. A prospective symptom diary for 2 cycles is required before diagnosis — retrospective reporting is insufficient.
questions.examStrategy: Differentiate: PMS (mild, life continues) vs PMDD (severe, impairs function, DSM-5 criteria) vs clinical depression (no cyclic pattern). PMDD treatment: SSRIs first-line (continuous or luteal-phase).
questions.keyTakeaway: PMDD: severe luteal-phase mood symptoms, resolves with menses, causes functional impairment → SSRIs first-line (can be luteal-phase dosing only).

questions.stem: A couple is expecting their first child. The father asks the nurse how the baby's sex was determined. Which explanation is most accurate?

options.A: The egg contains chromosomes that determine the sex; if the egg carries an X, the baby is female
options.B: The sex is determined at implantation, based on which hormone the uterus releases in response to the embryo
options.C: The father's sperm determines sex: sperm carrying X chromosome produces female offspring; sperm carrying Y produces male offspring
options.D: Sex is not genetically determined until week 7 of development, when gonads differentiate

questions.rationale: Sex is determined at fertilization. All ova carry an X chromosome (since the mother is XX). Sperm are either X-bearing or Y-bearing. If an X-bearing sperm fertilizes the egg → XX (female). If a Y-bearing sperm fertilizes → XY (male). The father's sperm is exclusively responsible for sex determination at the moment of fertilization.

incorrectRationales.A: All eggs contain an X chromosome — eggs do not carry Y chromosomes. The sex-determining chromosome comes from the sperm.
incorrectRationales.B: Sex is determined at fertilization by chromosomal content, not by hormonal uterine response at implantation.
incorrectRationales.D: The genetic sex is determined at fertilization (XX or XY). Gonadal differentiation begins at week 7 when the SRY gene on the Y chromosome triggers testicular development — but the genetic sex was set at fertilization.

questions.hint: Eggs always carry X. Sperm carries X (female) or Y (male). Sperm determines sex at fertilization.
questions.clinicalPearl: The SRY (sex-determining region Y) gene on the Y chromosome triggers the undifferentiated gonad to become a testis at week 7. Without SRY (XX embryo), the gonad defaults to ovary at week 12.
questions.examStrategy: Sex determination: occurs at FERTILIZATION. All eggs = X. Sperm = X or Y. Father determines sex. Commonly tested in genetics/embryology reproductive questions.
questions.keyTakeaway: Sex determined at fertilization: X-sperm → XX (female); Y-sperm → XY (male). Father's sperm = the sex-determining factor.

questions.stem: A client at 5 weeks gestation calls the clinic reporting light pink spotting that started yesterday, lasting about 24 hours, with no pain. Home pregnancy test remains positive. She is very anxious. Which response is most appropriate?

options.A: This is definitely implantation bleeding and she should not worry
options.B: She should come in immediately for an emergency evaluation for ectopic pregnancy
options.C: She should be evaluated with transvaginal ultrasound and serum β-hCG to distinguish between normal implantation spotting, threatened abortion, and ectopic pregnancy
options.D: Light bleeding in early pregnancy always means miscarriage; she should prepare for pregnancy loss

questions.rationale: Light bleeding in early pregnancy (5 weeks) requires evaluation, not dismissal or alarm. Implantation bleeding occurs around 20–24 days post-ovulation (before or around the time of the expected period). At 5 weeks, the timing is slightly late for implantation bleeding but possible. Differential includes: implantation bleeding (normal), threatened abortion, subchorionic hemorrhage, or ectopic pregnancy. TVUS + serial β-hCG are needed to characterize the pregnancy location and viability.

incorrectRationales.A: While implantation bleeding is possible, reassuring without evaluation is inappropriate — ectopic pregnancy must be excluded.
incorrectRationales.B: The clinical presentation (light, brief, pain-free bleeding with positive pregnancy test) does not indicate an emergency — but evaluation is warranted. 'Come in immediately' for emergency suggests hemodynamic instability, which is not described.
incorrectRationales.D: Light bleeding in early pregnancy is extremely common and frequently does not result in pregnancy loss. Approximately 20–25% of viable pregnancies have some first-trimester bleeding.

questions.hint: Early pregnancy bleeding = evaluate (TVUS + β-hCG). Don't reassure without excluding ectopic. Don't panic without hemodynamic instability.
questions.clinicalPearl: The most important step in evaluating first-trimester bleeding is to determine: (1) is the patient hemodynamically stable? (2) where is the pregnancy located (intrauterine vs ectopic)? (3) is there fetal cardiac activity?
questions.examStrategy: Early pregnancy bleeding questions: the answer is almost always 'evaluate' (TVUS, β-hCG). Avoid extremes (dismissal or emergency alarm) unless the clinical picture supports them.
questions.keyTakeaway: Early pregnancy bleeding: evaluate with TVUS + β-hCG. Don't reassure without ruling out ectopic. Don't catastrophize without hemodynamic compromise.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old female client is prescribed isotretinoin (Accutane) for severe cystic acne. The nurse is providing education. Which statement by the nurse is most important?

options.A: Use sunscreen while on isotretinoin due to increased photosensitivity
options.B: Avoid vitamin A supplements while on isotretinoin
options.C: Two forms of effective contraception must be used; monthly pregnancy tests required; do not become pregnant — fetal defects are severe and near-universal with exposure
options.D: Discontinue the medication if you develop joint pain, as this is a sign of toxicity

questions.rationale: Isotretinoin (a vitamin A derivative) is one of the most potent teratogens known — with nearly 100% incidence of major birth defects with 1st trimester exposure. Defects include microcephaly, CNS malformations, craniofacial defects, cardiac anomalies, and intellectual disability. The iPLEDGE REMS program mandates: monthly pregnancy testing (negative ≤30 days before each prescription), two forms of contraception for 1 month before, during, and 1 month after treatment. Patient, prescriber, and pharmacy must all be enrolled.

incorrectRationales.A: Photosensitivity is a real side effect but is not the most critical safety concern relative to the teratogenic risk.
incorrectRationales.B: Avoiding vitamin A supplements (which could cause additive toxicity) is true but secondary to the pregnancy prevention imperative.
incorrectRationales.D: Joint/muscle pain is a known side effect of isotretinoin, but it is not a sign of life-threatening toxicity. The critical teaching is pregnancy prevention.

questions.hint: Isotretinoin = most potent teratogen. iPLEDGE: 2 contraceptive methods + monthly pregnancy test + negative test before each fill.
questions.clinicalPearl: The iPLEDGE program requires a 23-day window: initial pregnancy test → wait 30 days → second test → 7-day window to pick up prescription. All parties (patient, prescriber, pharmacist) must document adherence in the registry.
questions.examStrategy: Isotretinoin questions: focus on pregnancy prevention. Two contraceptive methods + monthly pregnancy tests = the core nursing teaching. Teratogenic risk is the highest-priority concern.
questions.keyTakeaway: Isotretinoin: near-universal fetal defects with exposure. iPLEDGE: 2 contraceptive methods + monthly pregnancy tests. Absolute contraindication in pregnancy.
